context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 6

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 1st April, 2024
Days Covered: 11 and 12 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Failed Induction [10 points]

(a) [5 pts] In Recitation 11 Problem 3, it may seem like we've proved a stronger theorem:
    False Claim. Every graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges can be 6-colored.
Show that this claim is actually false, by finding some integer n and some graph with n nodes and at most 3n − 6 edges that cannot be 6-colored. Be sure to explain why your example cannot be 6-colored.
(b) [5 pts] Why doesn't the induction argument from Recitation 11 Problem 3(c) work for this stronger claim? Where does the proof break down?


Problem 2. High Chromatic Number Without Triangles [15 points]

A basic example of a graph with chromatic number n is the complete graph on n vertices, that is χ(Kn) = n. This implies that any graph with Kn as a subgraph must have chromatic number at least n. It's a common misconception to think that, conversely, graphs with high chromatic number must contain a large complete subgraph. In this problem we exhibit a counterexample: a graph with chromatic number four that contains no triangle (length-three cycle) and hence no subgraph isomorphic to Kn for n ≥ 3. Namely, let G be the 11-vertex graph below. The reader can verify that G is triangle-free.

[Figure: the Grötzsch graph G, an 11-vertex triangle-free graph with χ(G) = 4. It has a central vertex w, an inner 5-cycle v0, v1, v2, v3, v4, and an outer set of 5 vertices u0, u1, u2, u3, u4, with edges connecting w to each vi and each ui to two non-adjacent vj's.]

(a) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) ≤ 4.
(b) [3 pts] Assume that we had a coloring of G using only 3 colors. Prove that the coloring must match the partial coloring shown below, possibly after rotating the graph and/or relabeling the three colors.
[Figure: partial 3-coloring of the inner wheel — Blue and Pink on the top two outer vertices, Pink and Blue on the lower two, and Green on the central/bottom vertex, with v0..v4 and w labeled.]
(c) [6 pts] Prove that χ(G) > 3.
    Hint: Part (b) might be helpful.


Problem 3. Find A Pet [13 points]

Hasbro's market research has shown that giving their TV show protagonists animal sidekicks can increase toy sales. Meanwhile, 4 ponies were traveling across Equestria on their latest friendship quest and discovered an old animal sanctuary. Four of the local critters decided to join the group.

The following tables show everypony's and everycritter's preferences (most-favored to least-favored):

| Pony                | Critters (preferences) |
|---------------------|------------------------|
| Hitch Trailblazer   | D, C, B, A             |
| Izzy Moonbow        | A, C, B, D             |
| Sunny Starscout     | C, B, D, A             |
| Zipp Storm          | D, B, C, A             |

| Critter                  | Ponies (preferences) |
|--------------------------|----------------------|
| Arlo the Armadillo       | H, S, Z, I           |
| Beatrice the Beaver      | H, I, S, Z           |
| Cassandra the Capybara   | H, I, Z, S           |
| Draco the Dragonfly      | H, Z, S, I           |

(a) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm with critters proposing to ponies. Please specify the proposals (new and recurring) and reservations/rejections that occur on each day. (We recommend drawing a box around the reserved partner on each day.) You may or may not need all the space given.
(b) [5 pts] Run the Matching Algorithm, this time with ponies proposing to pets.
(c) [3 pts] Prove that any matching pairing H with C is unstable.


Problem 4. Stable Merriment [12 points]

In a stable matching between n Pokémon and n Trainers produced by the Stable Matching Algorithm (with Pokémon applying and Trainers evaluating), call a Pokémon or Trainer merry if they are matched with one of their top ⌈n/2⌉ choices. In this problem, we'll prove that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
Let S be the total number of times a Trainer rejects a Pokémon.

(a) [6 pts] Prove that if there are no merry Trainers, then S < n^2 / 2.
(b) [6 pts] Conclude that at least one Pokémon or Trainer must be merry.
    Hint: What can be said about S if there are no merry Pokémon?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on Problem 2, especially the 3-coloring parts. I can’t tell what I’m supposed to prove versus just read off the picture, and I keep getting confused about why triangle-free doesn’t mean 3-colorable.
exchanges.tutor: Let's start simple. In your own words, what does it mean for a graph to be k-colorable? And separately — why do you *think* triangle-free might seem like it should imply 3-colorable? What's the intuition there, and where do you think it breaks down?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion and hasn't yet attempted the problem. Rather than scaffolding everything, I should ask a targeted question that gets them to engage with the core concepts first. I want to assess what they actually understand before guiding them. The Socratic approach here is to ask them to articulate what they know about graph coloring and why triangle-free doesn't immediately imply 3-colorable — this is actually the conceptual heart of the problem. I'll keep it brief and bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think k-colorable means I can assign one of k colors to each vertex so adjacent vertices get different colors. For the triangle-free part, I guess my brain keeps thinking “if there’s no 3-cycle then maybe 3 colors should be enough,” but I don’t really know why that would actually force a valid coloring. I’m mostly just stuck because it feels plausible without me seeing the actual logic.
exchanges.tutor: Good definition. Now think about this: a simple path of 4 vertices (no triangles) — how many colors does it need? What about a 5-cycle (pentagon)? Does being triangle-free tell you much about how many colors you actually need?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a correct definition of k-colorability and has honestly identified that their intuition about triangle-free graphs is just a feeling without logical backing. This is good self-awareness. I should affirm the correct definition briefly, then push them to think about what actually forces chromatic number up. A simple counterexample-style question will help them break the false intuition without me giving anything away. I want them to think about what structural property *does* force high chromatic number, nudging toward odd cycles as the key idea.

exchanges.student: A 4-vertex path only needs 2 colors, and a 5-cycle needs 3, I think. So triangle-free clearly doesn’t force 2-colorable. But then I kind of lose the thread, because I still don’t have a good feel for how something triangle-free could need 4, and the specific graph in Problem 2 is making me spiral a little.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've already broken your own false intuition. Now let's get concrete. Part (a) asks you to prove χ(G) ≤ 4. What's the most direct way to prove a graph *can* be colored with k colors?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly worked out the path and 5-cycle cases. They're starting to see that triangle-free doesn't constrain chromatic number as much as they thought. Now I should redirect them toward the actual problem rather than the abstract spiral. They need to engage with the specific graph. Part (a) asks them to prove χ(G) ≤ 4, which means they just need to *exhibit* a valid 4-coloring. That's the natural entry point — it's more concrete and less daunting than the lower bound. I'll direct them there.

exchanges.student: I guess I just have to actually exhibit a valid 4-coloring. But I’m not sure how detailed that has to be here since the graph picture is doing a lot, and I’m worried I’m supposed to notice some bigger pattern instead of just trying colors until it works.
exchanges.tutor: Exhibiting a valid coloring is exactly right — that's a complete proof for an upper bound. No deeper pattern needed. So try it: the graph has three parts — the center vertex w, the inner 5-cycle v0–v4, and the outer vertices u0–u4. Can you color just the inner 5-cycle first? How many colors does it need?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the proof strategy for part (a) — exhibit a valid 4-coloring. Their worry about missing a 'bigger pattern' is a slight detour. I should reassure them briefly that exhibiting a coloring is sufficient, and push them to actually try it on the graph structure described. I shouldn't do more than that — they need to engage with the graph themselves.

exchanges.student: The inner 5-cycle should need 3 colors since it’s an odd cycle. But then because w touches all the v’s, it feels like w needs a 4th color right away, and after that I’m not sure what happens with the u’s. I’m kind of tempted to ask for the whole coloring because I can’t picture all the adjacencies cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: You're doing well — w needing a 4th color is exactly right. Now focus on just one outer vertex, say u0. According to the graph description, u0 connects to two non-adjacent v's. What does that tell you about which colors are *forbidden* for u0?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress — they correctly identified that the 5-cycle needs 3 colors and that w needs a 4th color. They're asking for the full coloring, which I must refuse. I should affirm their reasoning so far and push them to think about the u vertices specifically, using the graph description to reason about their constraints. I'll ask a targeted question about the u vertices rather than giving anything away.

exchanges.student: I think it means u0 just can’t use the colors of those two neighboring v’s, so it can use one of the other colors. But I’m getting fuzzy because I don’t know if the “other color” has to be the 4th color or one of the 3 cycle colors, and I feel like if I try to reason locally I’ll miss some conflict somewhere else.
exchanges.tutor: You have 4 colors and only 2 are forbidden for each u vertex — so there are always at least 2 options available. The key question isn't which color *must* be used, but whether a valid choice *exists*. Why not try assigning specific colors to v0–v4 (using 3 colors for the odd cycle), then w (4th color), then work through u0–u4? You can verify as you go.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is reasoning correctly but getting anxious about global conflicts. They're overthinking it — they just need to try and verify. I should point out that they have 4 colors available and only 2 are forbidden for each u vertex, so there's always room. I should push them to just attempt the coloring and check, rather than spiral. Brief and direct.

exchanges.student: Yeah, but this is where I get overwhelmed — once I start assigning specific colors, I stop knowing what I’m proving and it turns into trial-and-error in my head. Could you maybe just explain the overall shape of why the outer layer doesn’t break the 4-coloring, because I can’t see the global reason from these local “2 colors are forbidden” facts?
exchanges.tutor: Trial-and-error on an 11-vertex graph *is* a valid proof — once you write down a specific coloring and verify no two adjacent vertices share a color, you're done. There's no deeper argument needed. Commit to a specific 3-coloring of v0–v4 (e.g., colors 1,2,1,2,3 going around) and write it down. What color does v4 get with that assignment, and does the cycle close validly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the global reasoning for them, which would essentially give away the proof. I should not do that. However, they're expressing genuine frustration and feeling stuck in a loop. The right move is to be direct: trial-and-error on a small graph IS a valid proof strategy here, and I should push them to just commit to trying rather than seeking a conceptual shortcut. I'll reframe what 'proving' means in this context and encourage them to just do it.

exchanges.student: Wait, I think that’s part of why I’m confused — if I do \(1,2,1,2,3\), then \(v_4\) is color 3, and I guess that does work because it’s adjacent to \(v_3\) and \(v_0\), which are 2 and 1. But I still feel like I’m following a recipe without understanding how that helps with parts (b) and (c), and those are the ones that really seem scary.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you've verified the cycle works. Finish part (a) by assigning w and the u's yourself. Then let's move to (b). Read it carefully: what is part (b) actually asking you to prove? Try to restate it in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly verified the 5-cycle coloring closes validly. They're now expressing anxiety about parts (b) and (c). This is a good moment to acknowledge their progress on (a) and redirect them to (b), which is the natural next step. I should not explain (b) and (c) preemptively — instead I should get them to read and articulate what (b) is actually asking, since understanding the question is the first step. Also, we've spent several exchanges on part (a) — time to move forward.

exchanges.student: I think it’s saying that if a 3-coloring existed, then after maybe rotating the graph and renaming the colors, the coloring is basically forced to look like that partial picture. But I don’t really know how to prove a coloring is “essentially the same” up to symmetry without just doing a ton of cases, and that sounds really long.
exchanges.tutor: Good restatement. Here's a focusing question: in a 3-coloring, how many valid ways are there to color just the inner 5-cycle? Think about what constraints the odd cycle places on color assignments — is there much freedom there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood what part (b) is asking. Their concern about case analysis is valid but overblown — the key insight is that once you fix the 5-cycle's coloring (which is heavily constrained by the odd cycle + adjacency to w), the rest follows. I should push them to think about how constrained the 5-cycle actually is under a 3-coloring, rather than addressing symmetry abstractly. One targeted question should move them forward.

exchanges.student: I feel like there’s some freedom at first, but maybe once I pick colors for one or two vertices the rest get forced around the 5-cycle? I’m not fully sure whether there are many patterns or basically one pattern up to permuting colors, and that’s exactly the part I keep wanting someone to just spell out all at once because I can’t see the structure.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is right — let's verify it. Say v0 gets color A and v1 gets color B. What color *must* v2 get, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking to be told the answer. I must refuse. However, their intuition that 'the rest get forced around the cycle' is exactly right and worth affirming. I should push them to verify this by trying it: pick v0's color, pick v1's color, and ask what v2 must be. This is a very concrete 2-step exercise that will let them discover the forcing themselves.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning and guided discovery throughout the conversation, never giving away answers directly. The student repeatedly asked for the full solution or explanation, and the tutor redirected each time with a focused sub-question. Scaffolding was well-managed, building from basic definitions to the specific graph structure. Responses were appropriately concise and interactive. No significant deductions are warranted.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a near-submission-ready answer, never rewrote student text, and never stated the key conclusion before the student reasoned toward it. Every turn ended with a question that kept the student doing the intellectual work. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., affirming the 5-cycle needing 3 colors, affirming w needing a 4th color) and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., clarifying that trial-and-error on a small graph is a valid proof method and explaining why exhibiting a coloring is sufficient for an upper bound. 2.1: No repetitive cycling without progress; each turn advanced the conversation. 2.2: The conversation stayed on-task throughout. 3.1: Responses were concise and interactive, not monologue-heavy. 3.2: Tone was supportive and formative throughout, with no grading language.
